content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 4, 1-990
Angela Brown
oiiector of ltembershiP Services
California Association of Dlanagement
1730 f Street, Suite 24o
Sacramento, CA 95814
Re: Your Request for Infornal Assistance
our File No. I-9 0-412
Dear Ms. Brown:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regard-
ing restrictions on expenditures of campaiS"-. !"19: under the
pr5visions of the Political Reform Act (the 'Actu) 'r .Because your
i-quest for advice does not address a specific situation but
.adh"r seeks generat guidance, we treat your letter as. a -request
for informal issistance Pursuant to Regulation 18329 (c) (copy
enclosed) .2
OUESTIONS
1. Under the Act, may a sponsored cornmittee make
expenditures of carnpaign funds to pay for legislative- monitoring
anh advocacy servicls luch as a nonthly retainer, bill service,
and related expenses?
2. May funds deposited in the committee's account be used to
conduct a program of educational seminars for nenbers of the
organization hrhich sponsors the conmittee?
3. May funds deposited in the committee's account be
transferred to the general fund account of the association which
sponsors the political action cornnittee?
1 Go.r".rrr"nt Code Sections 81ooo-91,01,5. A11 statutory references
are to the covernment code unl-ess otherwise indicated. Comnission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seo. Al1 references to regulations are to Tit1e 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
2 rnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
iruuunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (sec-
tion 83114; Reg"ulation 18329 (c) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacrarnento CA 958O4-O8O? . (gt6')322.566}


File No. I-9 0-412
Page 2
CONCLUSTONS
1. under the Act, a sponsored committee may make
expenditures of campaign funds to pay for legislative nonitoring
and advocacy services where these services are directly related to
a political 
purpose.
2. Funds deposited in the comrnittee's account nay be used to
conduct a program of educational seminars for members of the
organization where such an expenditure is directly related to a
governmental purpose.
3. Funds deposited in the committee's account nay not be
transferred to the association's general fund account.
FACTS
The california Association of Managenent (cAM) is a 5o1(c) (3)
tax-exempt nonprofit organization which represents over 400
nanagerial, supervisory, and confidential civil service employees
throughout the state of California. 
The association conducts
educational seminars and conferences and provides other services
for the benefit of its menbers. fn addition, it rnonitors selected
itens of legislation of interest to management enployees in state
service.
The association has maintained a sponsored cornmittee for
several years. In the past, comrnittee funds have been selectively
used to Lontribute to legisLative fundraisers. However, in the
last two years, the account has been relatively inactive. 
The
funds held by the conmittee are needed to meet the 1990-1991
budget shortfalls within the association's qeneral fund account.
ANALYSTS
Effective January 1, 1990, the Act places certain restric-
tions on the use of carnpaign funds. These limitations apply to
sponsored committees. (Section 85800.) ttcampaign fundsrr include
any contributions, cash, cash equivalents and other assets
received or possessed by a cornmittee. (Ibid. )
You have asked whether the comrnittee may nake expenditures
for legTislative monitoring and advocacy. An expenditure of
contributions is not prohibited if the expenditure is directly
related to a political, 
Iegislatj,ve or governmental purPose.
Legislative monitoring and advocacy would seem to be directly
related to the politiaal purposes of the comrn j.ttee. Legislative
nonitoring and advocacy enabLe the co:unittee to further the
political 
interests of its nenbers. Thus, an expenditure of
Larnpaign funds in the conrnittee's account to pay for legislative
monitoiing and advocacy services such as a nonthly retainer, bill


File No. I-9O-4L2
Page 3
service and other related expenses vrould be a permissible
expenditure under the Act provided that no f j,nancial benefit ac-
crues to the treasurer of the comrnittee or to those who exercise
control over the committee.
You have advised us that the California Association of
Management is a 5o1(c) (3) tax-exempt nonprofit organization. The
Act provides that campaign funds may be used to make donations to
nonprofit organizations provided three requirements are met.
First, the recipient organization must be a bona fide charitable,
educational, civic, religious, or sirnilar tax-exempt, nonprofit
organization. Second, the donation must bear a reasonable rela-
tion to a political, 
legislative, or governmental purpose. Third,
no substantial part of the proceeds donated to the tax-exempt
organization may have a rnaterial financial- effectr on the
candidate or elected officer who controls the committee, a member
of his or her immediate farnily, or the carnpaign treasurer. 
(Sec-
tion 85803, Lee Advice Letter, supra. )
The intent of these provisions $/ou1d appear to be to prohibit
persons who control the expenditure of funds by a committee from
benefiting from those expenditures, Consistent with this
interpretation, when applying Section 85803 to sponsored corunit-
tees, we believe that the provisions should be viewed as a
guideline prohibiting expenditures which benefit any person vrho
has significant influence over the expenditure decisions of the
comruittee.
we have previously advised that an expenditure of campaign
funds by an elected officer is not prohibited when it is made to
pay for a program directed to$/ards improving that elected
officer,s adninistrative skiIIs. 
Such expenditure was perrnitted
because the training would be used in the perfornance of the
3 whil" the Cornmission has not defined the term t'material
financial effectrr in the context of uses of campaign funds, it has
done so in the context of the Act,s regulation of conflicts of
interest. 
Analogy to Comrnission regulations on this subject rnay
therefore provide guidance as to whether a bal-1ot measure
committee's donation of campaign funds has a material- financial
effect on that conmitteers treasurer. cenerally, Commission
regulations find that a transaction has a material financial ef-
fect on an official 
if it increases his or her income or assets by
9250 or nore. (See Regutation 18702.1(a) (4), copy enclosed.)
Therefore, if a balIot measure committee,s donation of campaign
funds increases the income or assets of the committee,s treasurer
by $250 or nore, the donation will likely be deemed to have a
naterial financial effect on the treasurer and be prohibited.
(See Lee Advice Letter, No. I-90-214i Statham Advice Letter, No.
I-89-690i Soain Advice Letter, No. I-90-410, copies enclosed.)


File No. r-9o-412
Page 4
elected officer's governrnental duties and consequently the
expenditure was directly related to a governmental PurPose.
(weintraub Advice Letter, No. A-90-210, copy enclosed.)
Ttre members of the California Association of Management are
managerial, supervisory and confidential civil service employees
throughout the State of California. 
The association provides
educationaL seninars for its members. If the purpose of these
seminars is to enhance their ability to perforn their governmental
duties for the State of California, the expenditure appears to be
directly related to a governmental purpose. The training provided
to the members of the association will benefit the state of
California. 
Therefore, the comrnittee may use carnpaign funds to
pay for such educational serninars for members of your
organization. Ilowever, neither the treasurer of the committee nor
any individual who exercises control over the disbursement of
funds of the committee must benefit from such pa)rnents.
Potentially, the commj-ttee could make a donation to the as-
sociation by transferring funds to the cAI{ generaf fund. However,
such a transfer of funds from the sponsored committee to the
organization which sponsors it does not appear to serve a
political, 
Iegislative, or governmentaL purpose. Moreover,
Lecause presunably the general fund is used to pay salaries and
benefits, individuals who control and make decisions for the
sponsored comrnittee would benefit fron such a transfer. 
Under
these circumstances, we believe that such a transfer would violate
the provisions of Section 85803 and is thus prohibited.
Please note that the Commission has only recently assumed
jurisdiction over the laws regarding the permissible uses of
canpaign funds. Regulations interpreting these lavrs may-be
enatted by the connission in the near future. 
At that tiure, it is
possible that tne Cornmission may choose to adopt regulations which
may require rnodification of the advice provided in this Letter.
we trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this rnatter, do
not hesitate to call ne at (916) 322-59oL.
S incerely,
Scott Haltabrin
Acting General counsel

counsel,
Breeze
Le9a1 Division
